title: Tapping for the Good Mother Pressure
subtitle: An EFT sequence for the relentless standard of good enough mothering
excerpt: An EFT tapping practice for the pressure of being a good mother: the constant evaluation, the standards that keep shifting, the sense that you are perpetually falling short of something. Works through the pressure toward being enough as you actually are.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. EFT framework developed by Gary Craig (mid-1990s).

practice.whenToUse: Use when the ongoing evaluation of whether you are a good enough mother is taking up significant mental space, or after an incident that has triggered strong self-criticism.

content.text: A five-minute EFT tapping practice for the good mother pressure. The script works through the constant internal audit of whether you are mothering correctly, the shifting goalposts of adequacy, and the exhaustion of being perpetually evaluated: by others and, more relentlessly, by yourself.
New to tapping? Read
how tapping works
first.
The practice
Karate chop
Tap the karate chop point and say each statement once:
Even though I feel like I am not getting this right and the bar keeps moving, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though I am tired of being evaluated and am doing most of the evaluating myself, I accept how hard this is.
Even though I am not the mother I thought I would be, I am the mother who is here every day.
Tapping round
Tap each point seven times while saying the phrase:
Eyebrow: The pressure to be the right kind of mother.
Side of Eye: The standard that was set somewhere and I can't seem to meet.
Under Eye: The constant running audit of today's performance.
Under Nose: The things I did wrong today that I am cataloguing.
Chin: How little my own needs get counted in this audit.
Collarbone: The exhaustion of the internal examiner.
Under Arm: The mother I actually am, right now, regardless of the standard.
Top of Head: Everything I have shown up for today.
Reframe to positive
Eyebrow: I am here. That is not nothing.
Side of Eye: My children need me to be present and regulated, not perfect.
Under Eye: The standard I am measuring against is not a real mother. It is a cultural construction.
Under Nose: I am allowed to end the audit for today.
Chin: A mother who shows up imperfectly and keeps going is doing the work.
Collarbone: I give myself the same compassion I would give another mother.
Under Arm: I am enough for today.
Top of Head: My children have me. That is what matters most.
Tap each round three times, then move to the reframe and tap that three times.
Where this practice comes from
Written for homemade.education. The EFT framework is Gary Craig's Emotional Freedom Technique, in use since the mid-1990s.
